2074331846641430736.headline: Mauricio Pochettino
2074331846641430736.body: The irony is that, for months, Mauricio Pochettino had been making every effort to ensure politics did not become a distraction for his United States side at this World Cup. Then the US President Donald Trump sticks his oar in and the co-hosts go from being a picture of concentration to a lost rabble. We may never quite know the extent to which the noise, chaos and controversy surrounding the suspension of Folarin Balogun’s red card affected the USMNT players’ state of mind in those 36 hours before they took to the field against Belgium. But, watching the US disintegrate over the course of an agonising 90 plus minutes at Lumen Field in Seattle, it felt like Trump had achieved the opposite of what he probably set out to do: Pochettino’s carefully orchestrated approach to keep politics out of the football torpedoed amid a grotesque orgy of political narcissism and grandstanding. Full report from Seattle ⬇️ telegraph.co.uk/football/202… Link Trump favour backfires as US exits World Cup with limp Belgium performance President scored a monumental own goal as home team’s bid for glory in the round of 16 came to a shuddering, humiliating halt telegraph.co.uk

2074366575818240469.headline: World Cup
2074366575818240469.body: Fallout from USMNT World Cup exit 🔺Iran revel in US’ defeat. “Now the whole world is dancing to celebrate politics’ humiliating defeat by football.” 🔺 Belgium players appear to poke a little fun at Trump by mimicking US President’s signature dance 🔺Official Belgium X account says “Overturn this” in a sarcastic dig at the Balogun episode 🔺Rudi Garcia praises Balogun for coming to talk to him: “It’s not his fault, he’s not the one to blame, I told him that.” 🔺Balogun: “When you know you're given a red card, and then you know usually the protocol is you don’t play in the following game, and then when that decision's overturned, of course it’s going to be controversial” (He’s been so classy Balogun throughout this furore) 🔺Pochettino says he’ll rest a bit and then in “the next weeks” discuss his future with US Soccer, who have already presented a proposal to extend his contract until the end of the 2030 WC ⬇️ telegraph.co.uk/football/202… Link ‘The world is dancing’: Iran mock US exit as Belgium do the Trump jig Middle East nation gloat as co-hosts crash out of the World Cup despite Fifa’s controversial decision to suspend Folarin Balogun ban telegraph.co.uk

Fallout from #USMNT World Cup exit
🔺Iran revel in US’ defeat. “Now the whole world is dancing to celebrate politics’ humiliating defeat by football.”
🔺 #Belgium players appear to poke a little fun at Trump by mimicking US President’s signature dance
🔺Official Belgium X account says “Overturn this” in a sarcastic dig at the Balogun episode
🔺Rudi Garcia praises Balogun for coming to talk to him: “It’s not his fault, he’s not the one to blame, I told him that.”
🔺Balogun: “When you know you're given a red card, and then you know usually the protocol is you don’t play in the following game, and then when that decision's overturned, of course it’s going to be controversial” (He’s been so classy Balogun throughout this furore)
🔺Pochettino says he’ll rest a bit and then in “the next weeks” discuss his future with US Soccer, who have already presented a proposal to extend his contract until the end of the 2030 WC

2074298455661658330.headline: Pierluigi Collina
2074298455661658330.body: Fifa referees’ chief Pierluigi Collina has jumped to the defence of Raphael Claus after the official at the centre of the Folarin Balogun controversy was branded “a little bit suspect” by Trump. “Raphael Claus is refereeing at his second FIFA World Cup having been with us in Qatar in 2022. He is an experienced and highly respected referee and we maintain full confidence in him as a trusted match official.”
2074298455661658330.raw_text:
Fifa referees’ chief Pierluigi Collina has jumped to the defence of Raphael Claus after the official at the centre of the Folarin Balogun controversy was branded “a little bit suspect” by Trump.

“Raphael Claus is refereeing at his second FIFA World Cup having been with us in Qatar in 2022. He is an experienced and highly respected referee and we maintain full confidence in him as a trusted match official.”

2074403293560746040.headline: Cristiano Ronaldo
2074403293560746040.body: Only eight men in history have scored more than Cristiano Ronaldo’s 11 World Cup goals. But relative to his brilliance, his longevity and his status in the sport, as the all-time record goalscorer in both club and international football, that is a frustratingly meagre return. For a sporting superhero, the World Cup has resembled kryptonite… But Ronaldo’s legacy and his story has been written over the past two decades. He should not spend the rest of his life fretting about the one that got away. OliverKay on Ronaldo, the World Cup and a question of legacy. 🔗 nytimes.com/athletic/7426532… Video
2074403293560746040.raw_text:
Only eight men in history have scored more than Cristiano Ronaldo’s 11 World Cup goals.

But relative to his brilliance, his longevity and his status in the sport, as the all-time record goalscorer in both club and international football, that is a frustratingly meagre return.

For a sporting superhero, the World Cup has resembled kryptonite…

But Ronaldo’s legacy and his story has been written over the past two decades. He should not spend the rest of his life fretting about the one that got away.

@OliverKay on Ronaldo, the World Cup and a question of legacy.

🔗 nytimes.com/athletic/7426532…

2074553657467826536.headline: Amadou Onana
2074553657467826536.body: 🚨 BREAKING: Belgium confirm Amadou Onana has ruptured his ACL. 🗣️ Team Doctor Brahim Hacene: “Unfortunately, the medical examinations have confirmed that Amadou has suffered a rupture of the anterior cruciate ligament (ACL). This is devastating news, both for him personally and for the team. “We immediately consulted with Amadou and with Aston Villa to agree on the best approach to his recovery. “Over the coming days, we will jointly determine the next steps in his medical treatment and rehabilitation. Throughout this process, we will continue to provide Amadou with our full support. In consultation with the player and Villa, it has been decided that Onana will remain with the squad at least until the end of the quarter-final.
2074553657467826536.raw_text:
🚨 BREAKING: Belgium confirm Amadou Onana has ruptured his ACL.

🗣️ Team Doctor Brahim Hacene: “Unfortunately, the medical examinations have confirmed that Amadou has suffered a rupture of the anterior cruciate ligament (ACL). This is devastating news, both for him personally and for the team.

“We immediately consulted with Amadou and with Aston Villa to agree on the best approach to his recovery. 

“Over the coming days, we will jointly determine the next steps in his medical treatment and rehabilitation. Throughout this process, we will continue to provide Amadou with our full support."

In consultation with the player and Villa, it has been decided that Onana will remain with the squad at least until the end of the quarter-final.

2074552941596508638.headline: Mauricio Pochettino
2074552941596508638.body: 🚨 US Soccer confirm positive conversations with Mauricio Pochettino about staying on. “We had positive conversations with Mauricio before the World Cup about the future. We agreed we would continue those conversations following a chance to rest and reflect post World Cup. We have a great deal of respect and gratitude for Mauricio, his staff and everyone part of the program. We have shared excitement about our potential and also shared clarity about the amount of work at all levels still required to achieve our ambition.”
2074552941596508638.raw_text:

We have a great deal of respect and gratitude for Mauricio, his staff and everyone part of the program. We have shared excitement about our potential and also shared clarity about the amount of work at all levels still required to achieve our ambition.”

2074551746639392801.headline: Flo Balogun
2074551746639392801.body: 🗣️ RBFA statement confirms the case with FIFA remains “ongoing” following Flo Balogun’s suspended red card. “RBFA remains committed to advocating for a review of the current FIFA regulations and their application “The Royal Belgian Football Association (RBFA) is proud of the way the Red Devils responded on the pitch yesterday. “At the same time, the RBFA will continue to pursue the ongoing FIFA matter off the pitch. The RBFA firmly believes that international football is best served by a disciplinary and governance framework that fully upholds the principles of legal certainty, transparency, equal treatment and fair play. “Clear procedures, respect for the rights of defence and the consistent application of the regulations are essential to maintaining the confidence of member associations, coaches, players, supporters and all other stakeholders in our sport. “Regardless of the sporting outcome, the RBFA will continue to advocate for the correct and consistent application of these principles by FIFA, ensuring that any form of arbitrariness is avoided. “In doing so, we feel supported by millions of football fans around the world, as well as by many other member associations. “Finally, on behalf of the coaching staff and the players, we would like to sincerely thank the Belgian supporters for their incredible and unwavering support throughout this FIFA World Cup. Now, together, our full focus turns to Friday's quarter-final against Spain.”
2074551746639392801.raw_text:
🗣️ RBFA statement confirms the case with FIFA remains “ongoing” following Flo Balogun’s suspended red card.

“RBFA remains committed to advocating for a review of the current FIFA regulations and their application

“The Royal Belgian Football Association (RBFA) is proud of the way the Red Devils responded on the pitch yesterday.

“At the same time, the RBFA will continue to pursue the ongoing FIFA matter off the pitch. The RBFA firmly believes that international football is best served by a disciplinary and governance framework that fully upholds the principles of legal certainty, transparency, equal treatment and fair play.

“Clear procedures, respect for the rights of defence and the consistent application of the regulations are essential to maintaining the confidence of member associations, coaches, players, supporters and all other stakeholders in our sport.

“Regardless of the sporting outcome, the RBFA will continue to advocate for the correct and consistent application of these principles by FIFA, ensuring that any form of arbitrariness is avoided.

“In doing so, we feel supported by millions of football fans around the world, as well as by many other member associations.

“Finally, on behalf of the coaching staff and the players, we would like to sincerely thank the Belgian supporters for their incredible and unwavering support throughout this FIFA World Cup. Now, together, our full focus turns to Friday's quarter-final against Spain.”

2074817226226479288.headline: Zlatko Dalic
2074817226226479288.body: Croatia manager Zlatko Dalic has stepped down after the national team’s World Cup exit in the round-of-32 stage. After his appointment in October 2017, Dalic led Croatia to the 2018 World Cup final against France. Four years ago in Qatar, Croatia followed up their 2018 run with a third-place finish, losing 3-0 to eventual winners France in the semi-finals having knocked Brazil in the last-eight stage. 🔗 nyti.ms/44ecNFJ
2074817226226479288.raw_text:
Croatia manager Zlatko Dalic has stepped down after the national team’s World Cup exit in the round-of-32 stage.

After his appointment in October 2017, Dalic led Croatia to the 2018 World Cup final against France.

Four years ago in Qatar, Croatia followed up their 2018 run with a third-place finish, losing 3-0 to eventual winners France in the semi-finals having knocked Brazil in the last-eight stage.

🔗 nyti.ms/44ecNFJ

2074829067468120359.headline: World Cup Diary
2074829067468120359.body: World Cup Diary, Day 25. I don’t believe in refereeing conspiracies. 2074905020630544406.headline: Bruno Guimarães
2074905020630544406.body: Arsenal pursuit of Bruno Guimarães ongoing. Although Newcastle insist no direct contact from Arsenal yet, a new bid is expected of around £60m. Arsenal informed via intermediaries last month that Newcastle don't want to sell, but Guimarães is keen on the move.🇧🇷 Ben Jacobs (JacobsBen) Arsenal prepared to make a formal bid to Newcastle for Bruno Guimaraes after seeing a verbal offer via intermediaries of around £55m turned down. Newcastle insist they don’t wish to sell, but direct club-to-club contact is now anticipated. As reported yesterday, Alex Scott, Ayyoub Bouaddi and Sandro Tonali other names on the radar. Matheus Fernandes has also been looked at, but Spurs and Manchester United more advanced as it stands. Overlap with other Premier League clubs for each target. MCFC want another midfielder post Elliot Anderson. Spurs also want to sign two midfielders. Manchester United will add at least one more midfielder. And Chelsea and Liverpool could also explore a central midfield reinforcement. More on talkSPORT.🎙️ 🤝 alex_crook —
2074905020630544406.raw_text:
Arsenal pursuit of Bruno Guimarães ongoing. Although Newcastle insist no direct contact from Arsenal yet, a new bid is expected of around £60m.

Arsenal informed via intermediaries last month that Newcastle don't want to sell, but Guimarães is keen on the move.🇧🇷


Ben Jacobs (@JacobsBen)

Arsenal prepared to make a formal bid to Newcastle for Bruno Guimaraes after seeing a verbal offer via intermediaries of around £55m turned down. Newcastle insist they don’t wish to sell, but direct club-to-club contact is now anticipated.

As reported yesterday, Alex Scott, Ayyoub Bouaddi and Sandro Tonali other names on the radar. Matheus Fernandes has also been looked at, but Spurs and Manchester United more advanced as it stands.

Overlap with other Premier League clubs for each target. #MCFC want another midfielder post Elliot Anderson.

Spurs also want to sign two midfielders.

Manchester United will add at least one more midfielder.

And Chelsea and Liverpool could also explore a central midfield reinforcement.

More on @talkSPORT.🎙️

🤝 @alex_crook





— https://nitter.net/JacobsBen/status/2070642858223120830#m

2074890419016552652.headline: Noel Aseko
2074890419016552652.body: 🚨⤵️ FC Bayern and Eintracht Frankfurt are in advanced negotiations over Noel Aseko. However, the deal is not done yet. The clubs are still negotiating the final transfer package, including the fixed fee, add-ons and whether a sell-on clause will be included. Florian Plettenberg (Plettigoal) 🚨🦅 EXCL | Eintracht Frankfurt are still pushing to sign Noel Aseko and have offered him a contract until 2030 + 1. No agreement has been reached yet, as Aseko has several offers on the table. The clear plan from Markus Krösche and Eintracht is not to link the deal to Nathaniel Brown. Two completely separate transfers. SGE FC Bayern open for a permanent top sale or another top loan move. Karl-Heinz Rummenigge, for example, is in favour of Aseko staying at the club. kerry_hau 🇩🇪🇬🇶 —

2075194651963728161.headline: Sean Steur
2075194651963728161.body: Newcastle United have completed the signing of midfielder Sean Steur from Ajax. Ajax were reluctant to sell the highly-rated midfielder but he entered the final two years of his contract, and there was no indication that he would renew his terms. Although Steur is not an exact like-for-like replacement for Sandro Tonali, as the Italian’s profile is unique, he would take the departed midfielder’s place in the squad. Steur is two-footed, has an element of press resistance in his play and can play deeper or more advanced in midfield. There is a feeling he has all the attributes to excel in the Premier League. More from GeorgeCaulkin 🔗 nyti.ms/4aL4iFM
2075194651963728161.raw_text:
Newcastle United have completed the signing of midfielder Sean Steur from Ajax.

Ajax were reluctant to sell the highly-rated midfielder but he entered the final two years of his contract, and there was no indication that he would renew his terms.

Although Steur is not an exact like-for-like replacement for Sandro Tonali, as the Italian’s profile is unique, he would take the departed midfielder’s place in the squad.

Steur is two-footed, has an element of press resistance in his play and can play deeper or more advanced in midfield. There is a feeling he has all the attributes to excel in the Premier League.

More from @GeorgeCaulkin 

🔗 nyti.ms/4aL4iFM

2075173125617033481.headline: Louie Barry
2075173125617033481.body: Aston Villa can confirm that Louie Barry has completed a permanent transfer to Sheffield Wednesday. Barry joined the club from Barcelona in 2020 and, after scoring on his senior debut against Liverpool in the FA Cup, would go on to lift the FA Youth Cup with Villa. He has since spent time on loan at Ipswich Town, Swindon Town, MK Dons, Salford City, Sheffield United, Hull City and Stockport County, enjoying three successful stints with the latter, helping the Hatters win promotion in 2024 and reach the League One Play-Off Final last term.
2075173125617033481.raw_text:
Aston Villa can confirm that Louie Barry has completed a permanent transfer to Sheffield Wednesday.

Barry joined the club from Barcelona in 2020 and, after scoring on his senior debut against Liverpool in the FA Cup, would go on to lift the FA Youth Cup with Villa.

He has since spent time on loan at Ipswich Town, Swindon Town, MK Dons, Salford City, Sheffield United, Hull City and Stockport County, enjoying three successful stints with the latter, helping the Hatters win promotion in 2024 and reach the League One Play-Off Final last term.

2075008534345896436.headline: Gareth Southgate
2075008534345896436.body: England’s camaraderie built under Gareth Southgate and been enhanced by Thomas Tuchel. Club England, Band of Brothers feel embodied in the dressing-room comedy double act of John Stones and Declan Rice, Manchester City and Arsenal, pranking Tuchel. “We go hard at it for 38 games of the season and those two they're at it all season for the league and then to come here, you completely forget about it,” says their England team-mate, Aston Villa’s Morgan Rogers. As he spoke in the England media bunker after training, Rogers was interrupted by Tottenham Hotspur’s Djed Spence with an urgent question: “Been a lot of speculation over who’s the fastest in the squad,” asked Spence. Rogers replied: “Let's just say I wouldn't bet against you in a race! But there's a couple saying they might have you. Anthony Gordon being one of them. A snapshot of the togetherness. Club cliques no more. Rogers: “You almost forget what clubs everyone plays for. It’s just everyone together as one. You can see that coming to show in games and in big moments, and then in the celebrations after with those two being silly!” The pranking – Stones pretending he’d injured his shoulder – came as England celebrated overcoming adversity in the Azteca on Sunday, going down to 10 men, and holding on to beat Mexico. Rogers: “Everyone can have good players and win games off ability, but we knew we had that difference of togetherness, grit, determination. “We showed the world we can win in different ways with different atmospheres, different environments. That gives the fans back home that extra belief that no matter how the game's going we've got the ability to find a way.” Rogers loves those special moments after the final whistle, standing in front of the fans, singing “Wonderwall”, sharing the moment and the joy of victory. “You're in awe of how many fans there are, how much it means and how passionate they are. It means everything to us.” Rogers is being heavily linked with a move to Arsenal. He’s too bright and respectful to be drawn on the subject. The 23-year-old talked, instead, of his desire to keep improving, to be more consistent, inspired by the high achievers around him with England. “I look at players in the room, in the training ground, the Harry Kanes, Judes. They've done it for five, six, seven, eight years consistently. That's the difference from a good player to a great player. I want to take that step. I want to be in those conversations as the best player in England and Europe. I’ve still got a long way to go and a lot of learning to do but I think I'm on the right path of learning from these players. I'm getting my consistency right.” Rogers also learns about consistency of performance from Ezri Konsa, a mentor at Villa. “He's a massive leader, a massive character here and at Villa. He's helped me in difficult moments this season, last season, just that quiet, calming word. He's never too high, never too low. He's just consistent, so he's a good person to lean on for me. “He's one of the best centre-backs at this World Cup. He never gets beaten, strong in the air, strong in the tackle, unbelievable on the ball. I honestly don't know a flaw in his game.” He’s under-rated. “That's helped Villa as probably a bigger club has taken him in the last few years. It's nice that he gets the recognition and he can compete on the world stage and everyone can how good he actually is. He does go under the radar massively.” With players of the calibre of Kane, Bellingham, and Konsa, and Rogers coming off the bench, the Villa attacker admits they are dreaming of winning the World Cup. “We're human and you can't help think that every game won is a step closer to the end goal - but if you take your eye off the ball for any second that's where you can get punished.” ENG AVFC FifaWorldCup Video
2075008534345896436.raw_text:
England’s camaraderie built under Gareth Southgate and been enhanced by Thomas Tuchel. Club England, Band of Brothers feel embodied in the dressing-room comedy double act of John Stones and Declan Rice, Manchester City and Arsenal, pranking Tuchel. “We go hard at it for 38 games of the season and those two they're at it all season for the league and then to come here, you completely forget about it,” says their England team-mate, Aston Villa’s Morgan Rogers. 

As he spoke in the England media bunker after training, Rogers was interrupted by Tottenham Hotspur’s Djed Spence with an urgent question: “Been a lot of speculation over who’s the fastest in the squad,” asked Spence. Rogers replied: “Let's just say I wouldn't bet against you in a race! But there's a couple saying they might have you. Anthony Gordon being one of them."

A snapshot of the togetherness. Club cliques no more.  Rogers: “You almost forget what clubs everyone plays for. It’s just everyone together as one. You can see that coming to show in games and in big moments, and then in the celebrations after with those two being silly!”

The pranking – Stones pretending he’d injured his shoulder – came as England celebrated overcoming adversity in the Azteca on Sunday, going down to 10 men, and holding on to beat Mexico. Rogers: “Everyone can have good players and win games off ability, but we knew we had that difference of togetherness, grit, determination. 

 “We showed the world we can win in different ways with different atmospheres, different environments. That gives the fans back home that extra belief that no matter how the game's going we've got the ability to find a way.” Rogers loves those special moments after the final whistle, standing in front of the fans, singing “Wonderwall”, sharing the moment and the joy of victory. “You're in awe of how many fans there are, how much it means and how passionate they are. It means everything to us.”

Rogers is being heavily linked with a move to Arsenal. He’s too bright and respectful to be drawn on the subject. The 23-year-old talked, instead, of his desire to keep improving, to be more consistent, inspired by the high achievers around him with England. “I look at players in the room, in the training ground, the Harry Kanes, Judes. They've done it for five, six, seven, eight years consistently. 

"That's the difference from a good player to a great player. I want to take that step. I want to be in those conversations as the best player in England and Europe. I’ve still got a long way to go and a lot of learning to do but I think I'm on the right path of learning from these players. I'm getting my consistency right.”

Rogers also learns about consistency of performance from Ezri Konsa, a mentor at Villa. “He's a massive leader, a massive character here and at Villa. He's helped me in difficult moments this season, last season, just that quiet, calming word. He's never too high, never too low. He's just consistent, so he's a good person to lean on for me.

“He's one of the best centre-backs at this World Cup. He never gets beaten, strong in the air, strong in the tackle, unbelievable on the ball. I honestly don't know a flaw in his game.” He’s under-rated. “That's helped Villa as probably a bigger club has taken him in the last few years. It's nice that he gets the recognition and he can compete on the world stage and everyone can how good he actually is. He does go under the radar massively.”

With players of the calibre of Kane, Bellingham, and Konsa, and Rogers coming off the bench, the Villa attacker admits they are dreaming of winning the World Cup. “We're human and you can't help think that every game won is a step closer to the end goal - but if you take your eye off the ball for any second that's where you can get punished.” #ENG #AVFC #FifaWorldCup

2074943277229310167.headline: Jude Bellingham
2074943277229310167.body: Jude Bellingham’s impact on this World Cup - three man of the match awards, four goals, one assist - hasn't surprised his long-standing friend and England team-mate Morgan Rogers: “I’m not surprised! Nobody in the squad is! We’re not surprised by how he has taken (control of) big key moments in games, in the space of five minutes (in the Azteca) he’s put his fingerprints on the game, he’s taken hold of the game. You need your big players in big games. You see his hunger to win, how much it means to him to decide these matches, and drive us forward. Rogers reckons the six-week break that the Real Madrid player had overcoming a hamstring problem helped him in getting ready for this. Rogers added: He was chomping at the bit to get going (here). Asked about how good can a player already world-class become, Rogers said of Bellingham: He can get even better, of course, he’s 23, his prime years are ahead of him. And with what he’s accomplished at such a young age. He knows he’s got things to work on, to improve. What is so exciting being an England fan, and his friend, is that there’s no cap on what he can achieve.” ENG

Nobody in the squad is! We’re not surprised by how he has taken (control of) big key moments in games, in the space of five minutes (in the Azteca) he’s put his fingerprints on the game, he’s taken hold of the game. You need your big players in big games. You see his hunger to win, how much it means to him to decide these matches, and drive us forward."

Rogers reckons the six-week break that the Real Madrid player had overcoming a hamstring problem "helped him in getting ready for this". Rogers added: "He was chomping at the bit to get going (here)." 

Asked about how good can a player already world-class become, Rogers said of Bellingham: "He can get even better, of course, he’s 23, his prime years are ahead of him. And with what he’s accomplished at such a young age. He knows he’s got things to work on, to improve. What is so exciting being an England fan, and his friend, is that there’s no cap on what he can achieve.” #ENG

2075112969524891913.headline: World Cup
2075112969524891913.body: In three out of every four years, FIFA loses money. In the fourth, the one with a men’s World Cup in, it makes that all back plus extra on top. FIFA’s 2023-26 revenue has been budgeted at a whopping $13billion (£9.7bn) — an increase of over 70 per cent from the previous four-year cycle. Of that $13bn, $9bn is forecast to arrive in 2026, with just about all of it attributable to the World Cup. Final figures will likely be higher. FIFA’s scope is dizzying. The aim of returning as much money as possible to football means huge costs. To go alongside $13billion revenues, FIFA, a not-for-profit entity, intends to spend $12.9bn, leaving just $100m to bolster reserves. Yet in each of the past two cycles, FIFA booked $1.2billion in surpluses, way in advance of its $100m budgets. Reserves already sat at $2.7bn at the end of 2025, even before this World Cup. FIFA’s cash pile has swollen to $1.2bn; the organisation holds almost $6bn in bonds, investments and deposits. An entity nearly laid to ruin by bribery and corruption just 11 years ago has more money sloshing around than ever. 📝 CWeatherspoon_ 🔗 nyti.ms/4vTzlrz Link The BookKeeper — Exploring FIFA’s finances: Where the money comes from and where it goes FIFA has never been richer. The World Cup has never been bigger. But at what cost? The Athletic investigates... nytimes.com
2075112969524891913.raw_text:
In three out of every four years, FIFA loses money. In the fourth, the one with a men’s World Cup in, it makes that all back plus extra on top.

FIFA’s 2023-26 revenue has been budgeted at a whopping $13billion (£9.7bn) — an increase of over 70 per cent from the previous four-year cycle. Of that $13bn, $9bn is forecast to arrive in 2026, with just about all of it attributable to the World Cup. Final figures will likely be higher.

FIFA’s scope is dizzying. The aim of returning as much money as possible to football means huge costs. To go alongside $13billion revenues, FIFA, a not-for-profit entity, intends to spend $12.9bn, leaving just $100m to bolster reserves.

Yet in each of the past two cycles, FIFA booked $1.2billion in surpluses, way in advance of its $100m budgets. Reserves already sat at $2.7bn at the end of 2025, even before this World Cup. FIFA’s cash pile has swollen to $1.2bn; the organisation holds almost $6bn in bonds, investments and deposits. An entity nearly laid to ruin by bribery and corruption just 11 years ago has more money sloshing around than ever.

📝 @CWeatherspoon_ 

🔗 nyti.ms/4vTzlrz

Link



The BookKeeper — Exploring FIFA’s finances: Where the money comes from and where it goes

FIFA has never been richer. The World Cup has never been bigger. But at what cost? The Athletic investigates...

2075284858373390601.headline: Pierluigi Collina
2075284858373390601.body: FIFA’s chief refereeing officer, Pierluigi Collina, has hit back at allegations that match officials can be influenced by external factors after suggestions that they are biased towards Argentina. His comments came after Hossam Hassan, the Egypt head coach, criticised referee Francois Letexier and said his side had been “treated unfairly” and “suffered injustice” during their round-of-16 defeat by Argentina on Tuesday. But Collina, a former World Cup final referee who joined FIFA in 2017, has strongly rejected the notion that his match officials are showing favouritism towards Argentina, or any other side. More from Dan_Sheldon_ 🔗 nyti.ms/4bx5UDe
2075284858373390601.raw_text:
FIFA’s chief refereeing officer, Pierluigi Collina, has hit back at allegations that match officials can be influenced by external factors after suggestions that they are biased towards Argentina.

His comments came after Hossam Hassan, the Egypt head coach, criticised referee Francois Letexier and said his side had been “treated unfairly” and “suffered injustice” during their round-of-16 defeat by Argentina on Tuesday.

But Collina, a former World Cup final referee who joined FIFA in 2017, has strongly rejected the notion that his match officials are showing favouritism towards Argentina, or any other side.

More from @Dan_Sheldon_ 

🔗 nyti.ms/4bx5UDe

2075460761304826243.headline: Kylian Mbappé
2075460761304826243.body: ✈️ A big private jet with five rooms. Kylian Mbappé and his family. Jürgen Klopp. 😱🎞️ Why Kylian and Klopp were 𝐩𝐨𝐢𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐚𝐭 𝐌𝐛𝐚𝐩𝐩𝐞’𝐬 𝐦𝐨𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫 and laughing yesterday? In 2017, the Reds were trying. The mission was to sign Mbappé. “𝐈 𝐭𝐚𝐥𝐤𝐞𝐝 𝐭𝐨 𝐋𝐢𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐩𝐨𝐨𝐥, 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐟𝐚𝐯𝐨𝐮𝐫𝐢𝐭𝐞 𝐜𝐥𝐮𝐛 𝐨𝐟 𝐦𝐲 𝐦𝐮𝐦; she loves them!”, Kylian said. Jürgen Klopp revealed all details on what really happened. 🇩🇪 “The 𝐦𝐨𝐬𝐭 𝐞𝐱𝐩𝐞𝐧𝐬𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐧𝐨𝐧-𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐬𝐟𝐞𝐫 𝐰𝐞 𝐢𝐧𝐯𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐝 𝐢𝐧. We flew from Blackpool to Nice. In Nice, the whole Mbappé family got on board a private plane with 𝐟𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐫𝐨𝐨𝐦𝐬 or something”. 🛫 “It was a very big plane. We really went big. Then we flew around in a circle, talked to the family and we ate good food”. “All of this happened… in the plane. We were 𝐍𝐎𝐓 allowed to be seen. So 𝐰𝐞 𝐟𝐥𝐞𝐰 𝐢𝐧 𝐚 𝐜𝐢𝐫𝐜𝐥𝐞. It was fantastic”, says Klopp. “We flew, ate, had good time… then 𝐡𝐞 𝐰𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐭𝐨 𝐏𝐚𝐫𝐢𝐬”, told Magenta TV. 😅
2075460761304826243.raw_text:
✈️ A big private jet with five rooms. Kylian Mbappé and his family. Jürgen Klopp. 😱🎞️

Why Kylian and Klopp were 𝐩𝐨𝐢𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐚𝐭 𝐌𝐛𝐚𝐩𝐩𝐞’𝐬 𝐦𝐨𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫 and laughing yesterday? In 2017, the Reds were trying.

The mission was to sign Mbappé. “𝐈 𝐭𝐚𝐥𝐤𝐞𝐝 𝐭𝐨 𝐋𝐢𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐩𝐨𝐨𝐥, 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐟𝐚𝐯𝐨𝐮𝐫𝐢𝐭𝐞 𝐜𝐥𝐮𝐛 𝐨𝐟 𝐦𝐲 𝐦𝐮𝐦; she loves them!”, Kylian said.

Jürgen Klopp revealed all details on what really happened. 🇩🇪

“The 𝐦𝐨𝐬𝐭 𝐞𝐱𝐩𝐞𝐧𝐬𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐧𝐨𝐧-𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐬𝐟𝐞𝐫 𝐰𝐞 𝐢𝐧𝐯𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐝 𝐢𝐧. We flew from Blackpool to Nice. In Nice, the whole Mbappé family got on board a private plane with 𝐟𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐫𝐨𝐨𝐦𝐬 or something”. 🛫

“It was a very big plane. We really went big. Then we flew around in a circle, talked to the family and we ate good food”.

“All of this happened… in the plane. We were 𝐍𝐎𝐓 allowed to be seen. So 𝐰𝐞 𝐟𝐥𝐞𝐰 𝐢𝐧 𝐚 𝐜𝐢𝐫𝐜𝐥𝐞. It was fantastic”, says Klopp.

“We flew, ate, had good time… then 𝐡𝐞 𝐰𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐭𝐨 𝐏𝐚𝐫𝐢𝐬”, told Magenta TV. 😅

🗣️J.B. Kerner: Ett så stort flygplan?

🗣️ Klopp: Ja, ja, vi slog verkligen på stort. Sen flög vi runt i en cirkel, pratade med familjen, åt god mat...

🗣️J.B Kerner: Vänta nu, ni ville inte bli sedda?

🗣️ Klopp: Vi fick inte bli sedda.

🗣️J.B Kerner: Ni fick inte bli sedda.

🗣️ J.B Kerner: De klev ombord och ni flög bara i en cirkel?

🗣️ Klopp: Vi flög i en cirkel. Det var fantastiskt. Och sedan gick han till Paris….

📽️MagentaTV

2075535881629221123.headline: Rudi Garcia
2075535881629221123.body: Rudi Garcia adds a maverick element to the Belgian setup. The former Marseille, Lyon and Napoli manager was four minutes away from his Belgium stint ending against Senegal. His contract is up at the end of the tournament and, having substituted Kevin De Bruyne and Jeremy Doku, his decisions would have been ridiculed had Belgium gone out. Instead, since the 86th minute against Senegal, they have scored seven goals (all with De Bruyne off the field) and looked a completely different team in defence and attack, producing incisive, fast-paced football. Garcia's team selections and unorthodox, unpredictable calls during games can raise eyebrows. He dropped De Bruyne and Doku against the U.S. and one backroom staff member, speaking anonymously to protect relationships, said: Sometimes even we are like, 'What the f*** is he doing?'. No one seems to know what he will do next. Garcia wasn't exactly popular with Belgium fans but since that Senegal match, a website has been set up where they can apologise to him. 'Sorry Rudi' had more than 10,000 apologies or comments posted on it as of Thursday. 📝 TimSpiers 🔗 nyti.ms/4gwSVF5
2075535881629221123.raw_text:
Rudi Garcia adds a maverick element to the Belgian setup.

The former Marseille, Lyon and Napoli manager was four minutes away from his Belgium stint ending against Senegal.

His contract is up at the end of the tournament and, having substituted Kevin De Bruyne and Jeremy Doku, his decisions would have been ridiculed had Belgium gone out.

Instead, since the 86th minute against Senegal, they have scored seven goals (all with De Bruyne off the field) and looked a completely different team in defence and attack, producing incisive, fast-paced football.

Garcia's team selections and unorthodox, unpredictable calls during games can raise eyebrows.

He dropped De Bruyne and Doku against the U.S. and one backroom staff member, speaking anonymously to protect relationships, said: "Sometimes even we are like, 'What the f*** is he doing?'." No one seems to know what he will do next.

Garcia wasn't exactly popular with Belgium fans but since that Senegal match, a website has been set up where they can apologise to him. 'Sorry Rudi' had more than 10,000 apologies or comments posted on it as of Thursday.

📝 @TimSpiers 

🔗 nyti.ms/4gwSVF5

2075211809015898482.headline: World Cup
2075211809015898482.body: 🇺🇸 From the inside read of the USMNT World Cup campaign... which really started when Mauricio Pochettino took over and found some culture shock: “The situation was worse than we really believed,” Pochettino said last month. Ahead of their first training camp in Austin, Texas, that October, he and his staff expected to meet players who “would be so desperate to help (and) come to the national team.” Instead, “we received a big punch,” he recalled. They felt they were the only ones excited for the World Cup to start. Christian Pulisic, the team’s star, left that camp early to return to AC Milan, a decision that was described as “load management” and roundly criticized by U.S. national team veterans, including Tim Howard and Alexi Lalas. Pochettino went along with that plan publicly, but it was symptomatic of dynamics that had to be fixed. He encountered players who had too much power, who dictated time off, and who sometimes used it for golf outings or meals with friends. Pochettino felt he had to strip away that power. The teardown was, at times, tumultuous. A March 2025 window, which culminated with losses to Panama and Canada, “was painful,” he’d later say. “But it was necessary … for the players to realize that (doing things) this way, it is impossible to arrive in a good condition for the World Cup.” A big read, with PaulTenorio and HenryBushnell nytimes.com/athletic/7429144… Link Inside the USMNT’s World Cup: BBQ bonding, squad’s shock over Balogun and exit ‘depression’ (Gift... Insiders reveal the story of a campaign that united the nation, sparked global controversy and ended in familiar heartache nytimes.com
2075211809015898482.raw_text:
🇺🇸 From the inside read of the USMNT World Cup campaign... which really started when Mauricio Pochettino took over and found some culture shock:

“The situation was worse than we really believed,”  Pochettino said last month. Ahead of their first training camp in Austin, Texas, that October, he and his staff expected to meet players who “would be so desperate to help (and) come to the national team.” Instead, “we received a big punch,” he recalled. They felt they were the only ones excited for the World Cup to start.

Christian Pulisic, the team’s star, left that camp early to return to AC Milan, a decision that was described as “load management” and roundly criticized by U.S. national team veterans, including Tim Howard and Alexi Lalas. Pochettino went along with that plan publicly, but it was symptomatic of dynamics that had to be fixed. He encountered players who had too much power, who dictated time off, and who sometimes used it for golf outings or meals with friends.

Pochettino felt he had to strip away that power. The teardown was, at times, tumultuous. A March 2025 window, which culminated with losses to Panama and Canada, “was painful,” he’d later say. “But it was necessary … for the players to realize that (doing things) this way, it is impossible to arrive in a good condition for the World Cup.”

A big read, with @PaulTenorio and  @HenryBushnell 
nytimes.com/athletic/7429144…

2075617249088725477.headline: Vinicius Jr
2075617249088725477.body: 🟡🟢 Vinicius Jr statement. “Almost four years later and once again thinking about what to write after a frustration in the World Cup. I saw so many people of all ages supporting me, embracing our dream, that it would be unfair to remain silent. But I needed a few days to reflect. Wearing the national team jersey is the greatest pride of my life, and leaving a World Cup in the round of sixteen is a feeling that's hard to explain. I know how much I prepared, how much I focused, how much I wanted this for you, for my family. The feeling of frustration is absurd. We had a group strong enough to do more, and we couldn't. I apologize and I'm going to fight for our dream of returning to the top of the world.”
2075617249088725477.raw_text:
🟡🟢 Vinicius Jr statement.

“Almost four years later and once again thinking about what to write after a frustration in the World Cup.

I saw so many people of all ages supporting me, embracing our dream, that it would be unfair to remain silent. 

But I needed a few days to reflect.

Wearing the national team jersey is the greatest pride of my life, and leaving a World Cup in the round of sixteen is a feeling that's hard to explain.

I know how much I prepared, how much I focused, how much I wanted this for you, for my family.

The feeling of frustration is absurd. We had a group strong enough to do more, and we couldn't.

I apologize and I'm going to fight for our dream of returning to the top of the world.”

2075573281269362980.headline: Richard Hughes
2075573281269362980.body: More on Michael Edwards' departure as FSG's CEO of Football. Understand Edwards informed FSG he was leaving last autumn when it was clear they were not expanding their football portfolio. However, Edwards chose to stay on and support Richard Hughes. Edwards never wanted to return in a recruitment role and the main appeal was driving the purchase of a new club. Bordeaux was initially looked it and Getafe explored. Once plans to buy the latter stalled, Edwards' exit was inevitable. The role Edwards fulfilled became very different to the one he’d been promised. He will now be in demand and is unlikely to seek another extended break from football.
2075573281269362980.raw_text:
More on Michael Edwards' departure as FSG's CEO of Football.

Understand Edwards informed FSG he was leaving last autumn when it was clear they were not expanding their football portfolio. However, Edwards chose to stay on and support Richard Hughes.

Edwards never wanted to return in a recruitment role and the main appeal was driving the purchase of a new club. Bordeaux was initially looked it and Getafe explored. Once plans to buy the latter stalled, Edwards' exit was inevitable.

The role Edwards fulfilled became very different to the one he’d been promised. 

He will now be in demand and is unlikely to seek another extended break from football.

2075568668054798600.headline: Fenway Sports Group
2075568668054798600.body: Michael Edwards has stepped down as Fenway Sports Group’s CEO of football. When Edwards, who worked as Liverpool’s sporting director between 2016 and 2022, agreed to return to the fold to oversee the post-Jurgen Klopp era two years ago, he made it clear that FSG’s commitment to embarking on a multi-club model had been crucial in convincing him to accept the newly created role of CEO of football. However, despite leading an extensive search, with analysis conducted on around 25 clubs across Europe with a strong focus on Spain, Portugal and France, no proposal got the green light from the FSG board. 📝 JamesPearceLFC 🔗 nyti.ms/4wzKw8u
2075568668054798600.raw_text:
Michael Edwards has stepped down as Fenway Sports Group’s CEO of football.

When Edwards, who worked as Liverpool’s sporting director between 2016 and 2022, agreed to return to the fold to oversee the post-Jurgen Klopp era two years ago, he made it clear that FSG’s commitment to embarking on a multi-club model had been crucial in convincing him to accept the newly created role of CEO of football.

However, despite leading an extensive search, with analysis conducted on around 25 clubs across Europe with a strong focus on Spain, Portugal and France, no proposal got the green light from the FSG board.

📝 @JamesPearceLFC

2075638800462778668.headline: Kevin De Bruyne
2075638800462778668.body: Kevin De Bruyne is back for Belgium, having been left out completely for the 4-1 win over the U.S. It's no surprise to see him return given Amadou Onana's injury, but also with the game against Spain likely to be played at a slightly more sedate pace if, as expected, Luis de la Fuente's side dominate possession. A couple of games this tournament have passed the ageing De Bruyne by, but perhaps today could be his Last Dance. Jeremy Doku also returns to the Belgium side (he was on the bench against the U.S.) but Romelu Lukaku continues in his super-sub role. Manager Rudi Garcia spoke this week about having a starting XI and a group of finishers, Lukaku being the prime example. Belgium have scored six goals beyond the 85th minute in their last three matches. 📝 TimSpiers Spain vs Belgium live blog: bit.ly/4h4P7Lo
2075638800462778668.raw_text:
Kevin De Bruyne is back for Belgium, having been left out completely for the 4-1 win over the U.S. It's no surprise to see him return given Amadou Onana's injury, but also with the game against Spain likely to be played at a slightly more sedate pace if, as expected, Luis de la Fuente's side dominate possession.

A couple of games this tournament have passed the ageing De Bruyne by, but perhaps today could be his "Last Dance".

Jeremy Doku also returns to the Belgium side (he was on the bench against the U.S.) but Romelu Lukaku continues in his super-sub role. Manager Rudi Garcia spoke this week about having a starting XI and a group of finishers, Lukaku being the prime example.

Belgium have scored six goals beyond the 85th minute in their last three matches.

📝 @TimSpiers

Spain vs Belgium live blog: bit.ly/4h4P7Lo

2075695291697688688.headline: World Cup
2075695291697688688.body: It’s France versus Spain in the semi-final of the World Cup in Dallas on Tuesday, a meeting of arguably the best two teams in the competition. This seems to happen at every World Cup, a ‘final’ but played out at an earlier stage. It will be pitched as the unstoppable force of France meeting the immovable object of Spain. Didier Deschamps’ team undoubtedly possess the competition’s best attack, with Kylian Mbappe, Ousmane Dembele, Michael Olise and Desire Doue forming a formidable front line that looks almost impossible for any defence to resist. If anyone can, it’s probably Spain. There is also a school of thought that perhaps the best way to subdue or nullify France is by, well, not letting them have the ball and for all the magic of Lionel Messi and the gripping football of Argentina, Spain are, again, probably the best team in the tournament for that. Either way, it has all the hallmarks of a classic World Cup encounter. And the victor will be favourites to lift the trophy next weekend, regardless of who they face in the final. 📝 TimSpiers bit.ly/4h4qzCf
2075695291697688688.raw_text:
It’s France versus Spain in the semi-final of the World Cup in Dallas on Tuesday, a meeting of arguably the best two teams in the competition.

This seems to happen at every World Cup, a ‘final’ but played out at an earlier stage.

It will be pitched as the unstoppable force of France meeting the immovable object of Spain.

Didier Deschamps’ team undoubtedly possess the competition’s best attack, with Kylian Mbappe, Ousmane Dembele, Michael Olise and Desire Doue forming a formidable front line that looks almost impossible for any defence to resist.

If anyone can, it’s probably Spain.

There is also a school of thought that perhaps the best way to subdue or nullify France is by, well, not letting them have the ball and for all the magic of Lionel Messi and the gripping football of Argentina, Spain are, again, probably the best team in the tournament for that.

Either way, it has all the hallmarks of a classic World Cup encounter. And the victor will be favourites to lift the trophy next weekend, regardless of who they face in the final.

📝 @TimSpiers

bit.ly/4h4qzCf

2075750847875624982.headline: Harry Kane
2075750847875624982.body: Harry Kane on the debate about appointing a foreign coach in Thomas Tuchel. “Whoever gets appointed to be England manager, there’s a debate. It never crossed my mind that he wasn’t English. We had foreign coaches, we've had English coaches. Ultimately, we’re just trying to be successful as a team. We want to win major tournaments. Knowing the boss from Bayern Munich, I knew he was going to give us a great chance of doing that. I was excited to work with him again.”
2075750847875624982.raw_text: Harry Kane on the debate about appointing a foreign coach in Thomas Tuchel. “Whoever gets appointed to be England manager, there’s a debate. It never crossed my mind that he wasn’t English. We had foreign coaches, we've had English coaches. Ultimately, we’re just trying to be successful as a team. We want to win major tournaments. Knowing the boss from Bayern Munich, I knew he was going to give us a great chance of doing that. I was excited to work with him again.”

2075746204546166949.headline: Harry Kane
2075746204546166949.body: Harry Kane gives insight into how he maintains his astonishing goal return (73 goals in 63 games for Bayern and England this season). “Throughout any career, you have to have little motivations, targets and goals. I like to take a month or six weeks at a time, see how many goals I can reach, and set myself small targets throughout the season. Obviously, this season has gone very well on that front, but more games to play still. That's what keeps me hungry. pushing myself to the highest level. To see how far I can get in that small space of time.”
2075746204546166949.raw_text: Harry Kane gives insight into how he maintains his astonishing goal return (73 goals in 63 games for Bayern and England this season). “Throughout any career, you have to have little motivations, targets and goals. I like to take a month or six weeks at a time, see how many goals I can reach, and set myself small targets throughout the season. Obviously, this season has gone very well on that front, but more games to play still. That's what keeps me hungry. pushing myself to the highest level. To see how far I can get in that small space of time.”

2075901590058225833.headline: Thibaut Courtois
2075901590058225833.body: Belgium goalkeeper Thibaut Courtois said after his nation’s World Cup exit that he wants to take a “break” from international football, but said that the decision ultimately lies with the Belgian FA. Spain defeated Belgium 2-1 on Friday after an 88th-minute winner from Mikel Merino. Courtois was substituted in the 71st minute after appearing to sustain a leg muscle injury and was replaced by Senne Lammens, who was at fault for Spain’s winner. “As for my future with the national team, we’ll see,” he told reporters. “I’d perhaps like to take a break from the Nations League, as it’s not the most important thing. And maybe come back for the Euro qualifiers. “But ultimately, it’s a decision the football association has to make. If not, perhaps today was my last match.” More from samjoseph_17 🔗 nyti.ms/4vXGvuV
2075901590058225833.raw_text:
Belgium goalkeeper Thibaut Courtois said after his nation’s World Cup exit that he wants to take a “break” from international football, but said that the decision ultimately lies with the Belgian FA.

Spain defeated Belgium 2-1 on Friday after an 88th-minute winner from Mikel Merino. Courtois was substituted in the 71st minute after appearing to sustain a leg muscle injury and was replaced by Senne Lammens, who was at fault for Spain’s winner.

“As for my future with the national team, we’ll see,” he told reporters. “I’d perhaps like to take a break from the Nations League, as it’s not the most important thing. And maybe come back for the Euro qualifiers.

“But ultimately, it’s a decision the football association has to make. If not, perhaps today was my last match.”

More from @samjoseph_17 

🔗 nyti.ms/4vXGvuV

2075937358726471919.headline: Red Bull
2075937358726471919.body: Jurgen Klopp has verbally agreed a deal to become Germany coach on a four-year contract. Parties now expected to formalise next week, including an exit package with Red Bull. Pep Lijnders also set to join at Klopp's assistant.🇩🇪 🗣️ DFB Statement: DFB President Bernd Neuendorf and DFB Vice President Hans-Joachim Watzke held their first in-depth talks with Jürgen Klopp yesterday in New York regarding his potential appointment as national team coach. During the constructive exchange, an agreement was reached on the key points of a potential contract. Talks will continue next week. Both sides are confident that the negotiations – subject to an agreement with Klopp's current employer, Red Bull – can ultimately be successfully concluded. Any potential contract must be finalised in a joint meeting of the supervisory board and shareholders' meeting of DFB GmbH & Co. KG. Ben Jacobs (JacobsBen) Jurgen Klopp now close to formalising an agreement to take over as Germany boss until summer 2030. Klopp has already verbally agreed to the role.🇩🇪 —
2075937358726471919.raw_text:
Jurgen Klopp has verbally agreed a deal to become Germany coach on a four-year contract. Parties now expected to formalise next week, including an exit package with Red Bull. Pep Lijnders also set to join at Klopp's assistant.🇩🇪

🗣️ DFB Statement: "DFB President Bernd Neuendorf and DFB Vice President Hans-Joachim Watzke held their first in-depth talks with Jürgen Klopp yesterday in New York regarding his potential appointment as national team coach. During the constructive exchange, an agreement was reached on the key points of a potential contract. Talks will continue next week.

Jurgen Klopp now close to formalising an agreement to take over as Germany boss until summer 2030.

2075939884234149998.headline: Ken Bates
2075939884234149998.body: Ken Bates, former chairman of Chelsea and Leeds United, has died aged 94. His first formal role in football was as chairman of Oldham Athletic for five years in the 1960s. He later had a spell as co-owner and vice chairman of Wigan Athletic from 1980 to 1982. But it is Chelsea with whom Bates is most readily associated. He bought the club for £1 in 1982, assuming the club’s debts, fighting a long-running battle with property developers over the fate of Stamford Bridge and building the club into a regular presence in the Premier League’s top half before selling to Roman Abramovich in a deal worth £140million ($187.6m at current rates) in July 2003. liam_twomey and SJohnsonSport
2075939884234149998.raw_text:
Ken Bates, former chairman of Chelsea and Leeds United, has died aged 94.

His first formal role in football was as chairman of Oldham Athletic for five years in the 1960s. He later had a spell as co-owner and vice chairman of Wigan Athletic from 1980 to 1982.

But it is Chelsea with whom Bates is most readily associated.

He bought the club for £1 in 1982, assuming the club’s debts, fighting a long-running battle with property developers over the fate of Stamford Bridge and building the club into a regular presence in the Premier League’s top half before selling to Roman Abramovich in a deal worth £140million ($187.6m at current rates) in July 2003.

@liam_twomey and @SJohnsonSport

2075936767593611695.headline: Alejandro Garnacho
2075936767593611695.body: Chelsea’s Alejandro Garnacho has not reported back for pre-season training as he looks to secure a move away from the club. Garnacho joined Chelsea from Manchester United for £40million last summer but the 22-year-old is now keen to leave Stamford Bridge in order to get regular first-team football. Sources close to Chelsea said the club is only considering selling the Argentina international on a permanent basis rather than sanctioning a loan deal and value him at £42.5m (€50m). More from SJohnsonSport and nnamdionye 🔗 nyti.ms/3SSTUWp

2075968935267082587.headline: Ken Bates
2075968935267082587.body: Ken Bates saved Chelsea. Not only back in 1982 when Bates rescued the club from potential oblivion. But still now, even following the sad news of his passing aged 94, Bates’ legacy continues to protect Chelsea. He created the CPO, Chelsea Pitch Owners, which safeguards the future of the stadium. Bates achieved much in an extraordinary life. He fought to protect the FA Cup, fought for the new Wembley to be built, stabilised Leeds United and fought for smaller clubs but his greatest legacy revolves around Chelsea. Combative, controversial, loyal and principled in his way, Bates was driven by a desire to prove himself, to make something of himself, to take on the establishment in its varied forms. Much of this drive stemmed from memories of growing up without parents (his mother died, his father disappeared and he was raised by his grandparents) and frustration at a childhood disability destroying his dream of making it as a footballer at Arsenal. He'd been a good attacker. It was April 2 1982, when Bates rescued Chelsea. He spoke to the club bankers who’d run out of patience. “We have two cheques here,” the bank told Bates. “One is for the share of the (FA Cup) gate which has to go to the FA. And one is the players’ wages. Which one can we bounce?” He came in, invested money to tide the club over and started cutting costs. Entering the Bridge boardroom for the first time pre-match, Bates was shocked to see the number of guests, the feast laid on, even a cigar box being passed around at a club losing £12,000 a week. Chelsea even had a club chauffeur. Not for long. Bates’ cost-cutting was brutal but vital. Chelsea recovered, fought off property developers through Bates’ strength of personality, established themselves as a force in the Premier League and became more involved in European competition. Bates loved Chelsea. He loved entertainers. His favourite was Gianfranco Zola because the Italian played with a smile, dribbled and lit up stadiums and fans’ lives. Even after selling to Roman Abramovich in 2003, taking over Leeds where his cost-cutting (and Chelsea past) alienated many fans, and then living in retirement with his beloved Suzannah in Monaco, Bates would try and return to watch matches at the Bridge. He kept a flat full of mementos nearby. On the sideboard was a framed copy of Bates’ CPO share. One day while Chelsea chairman, a fan shouted about the possibility of “your grandson sells the land for houses”. Bates’ initial reaction was, typically, “You ungrateful bastard”. But he realised that was a very real threat – the Bridge was prime London land. Developers craved it. So Bates created the CPO; it now numbers 13,000 fans owning 23,000 individual shares (Thomas Tuchel has one) and a large majority would be required to vote for any change in use of the pitch. And they aren’t for change. As Bates intended. We last spoke on May 18. Ken sounded frail but was typically opinionated on the footballing issues of the day. Bates had his difficult side, vendettas and views, but he was passionate about family, football and Chelsea FC. RIP, Ken.

2076107374943666591.headline: Former Premier League
2076107374943666591.body: Former Premier League referee Graham Scott (refsplaining) on the controversy surrounding Jude Bellingham's first goal against Norway: The Skycam attached to cables that hovers above the pitch may have played an unwitting part in Jude Bellingham’s goal just before half-time. The on-field team would only spot such contact if the ball’s trajectory was affected dramatically. The VAR could intervene if there was evidence one of the teams suffered an obvious disadvantage, but that has to be clear and obvious. Strictly speaking, you can make a case for outside interference, which means there should be a dropped ball. But in practice there has to be some evidence of impact. You would allow play to continue if the ball hit a paper cup or small piece of debris on the pitch if it made no difference to the play. It’s hard to be sure here, but in any event it’s unlikely the VAR would even consider checking a camera angle up in the gods when reviewing the attacking phase. He would have been focusing on the actions of the players in the build-up, and there was no obvious reason to dig so deep, especially given the pressure on all video officials not to be too forensic, nor to delay the game in an apparent quest to find something wrong with what appeared to be a perfectly valid goal. Norway 1-2 England reaction 🔗 bit.ly/4w3RvXy
2076107374943666591.raw_text:
Former Premier League referee Graham Scott (@refsplaining) on the controversy surrounding Jude Bellingham's first goal against Norway:

"The Skycam attached to cables that hovers above the pitch may have played an unwitting part in Jude Bellingham’s goal just before half-time.

"The on-field team would only spot such contact if the ball’s trajectory was affected dramatically. The VAR could intervene if there was evidence one of the teams suffered an obvious disadvantage, but that has to be clear and obvious.

"Strictly speaking, you can make a case for outside interference, which means there should be a dropped ball. But in practice there has to be some evidence of impact.

"You would allow play to continue if the ball hit a paper cup or small piece of debris on the pitch if it made no difference to the play. It’s hard to be sure here, but in any event it’s unlikely the VAR would even consider checking a camera angle up in the gods when reviewing the attacking phase.

"He would have been focusing on the actions of the players in the build-up, and there was no obvious reason to dig so deep, especially given the pressure on all video officials not to be too forensic, nor to delay the game in an apparent quest to find something wrong with what appeared to be a perfectly valid goal."

Norway 1-2 England reaction 🔗 bit.ly/4w3RvXy

2076144051137454494.headline: Breel Embolo
2076144051137454494.body: The problem with the new ‘mistaken identity’ rule is that it sets a dangerous precedent. Breel Embolo dived, so if called on the field it’s a second yellow, but that wasn’t the decision. Yet an amendment to IFAB wording before the tournament states: “When the referee shows a yellow or red card but has clearly penalised the wrong player of either team for the offence in question; the offence itself cannot be reviewed except in the context of mistaken identity.” We saw the same with Tim Ream (originally booked for a foul) and Miguel Almiron (eventually booked for diving) in USA vs. Paraguay. In both instances, the right decision was reached when revisiting the incidents. Yet it’s massively open for debate as to whether this is actually ‘mistaken identity’, and that’s the issue. It’s just a mistake by the referees. Both got sold by a dive. They didn’t mix up players. They just made a bad call on a regular foul and wrongly handed out a yellow card. So really it’s just a rule allowing VAR to intervene on more minor on-field calls, and that’s the part a lot of people will feel uneasy about.
2076144051137454494.raw_text:
The problem with the new ‘mistaken identity’ rule is that it sets a dangerous precedent.

Breel Embolo dived, so if called on the field it’s a second yellow, but that wasn’t the decision.

Yet an amendment to IFAB wording before the tournament states: “When the referee shows a yellow or red card but has clearly penalised the wrong player of either team for the offence in question; the offence itself cannot be reviewed except in the context of mistaken identity.”

We saw the same with Tim Ream (originally booked for a foul) and Miguel Almiron (eventually booked for diving) in USA vs. Paraguay.

In both instances, the right decision was reached when revisiting the incidents.

Yet it’s massively open for debate as to whether this is actually ‘mistaken identity’, and that’s the issue. 

It’s just a mistake by the referees. Both got sold by a dive. They didn’t mix up players. They just made a bad call on a regular foul and wrongly handed out a yellow card.

So really it’s just a rule allowing VAR to intervene on more minor on-field calls, and that’s the part a lot of people will feel uneasy about.

2076260531195858986.headline: Erling Haaland
2076260531195858986.body: If I try to think quickly through these 40 days, it has been absolutely insane, the coolest thing I’ve ever been a part of. Erling Haaland said that he felt the World Cup experience “changes Norway … and it changes me”. So much about modern football seems so terribly serious. The Premier League can be like that, a multi-billion-pound entertainment product played under largely grey skies in what can at times feel like a mood of austerity. But the World Cup — this World Cup, and particularly Haaland’s World Cup — has looked like… well, a show or a rollercoaster ride, like he said. 📝 OliverKay 🔗 nyti.ms/4yirYvi
2076260531195858986.raw_text:

But the World Cup — this World Cup, and particularly Haaland’s World Cup — has looked like… well, a show or a rollercoaster ride, like he said.

2076245557475962897.headline: World Cup
2076245557475962897.body: Using in-house data, FIFA’s Football Performance Insights team have noticed a trend at this summer's World Cup. Compared to previous editions, they have spotted that possessions including an off-ball run which targets the inside channels and the space in behind the opposition defence are leading to successful actions more frequently. In other words, attacking the gap between the widest defender and the centre-back nearest to them with a forward run is increasingly valuable. Compared to the previous World Cup four years ago, possession sequences that include such a movement in the 2026 tournament are leading to around 2.7 shots on goal per 30 minutes of ball-in-play time — an increase of around 34 per cent. Those runs are effective because they cause tension in the opponents’ defence. Most often, that full-back will have their eye on a winger, while the centre-back on that side will be tracking the striker. ThomHarris_ 🔗 nyti.ms/4yiUz3t
2076245557475962897.raw_text:
Using in-house data, FIFA’s Football Performance Insights team have noticed a trend at this summer's World Cup. 

Compared to previous editions, they have spotted that possessions including an off-ball run which targets the inside channels and the space in behind the opposition defence are leading to successful actions more frequently.

In other words, attacking the gap between the widest defender and the centre-back nearest to them with a forward run is increasingly valuable.

Compared to the previous World Cup four years ago, possession sequences that include such a movement in the 2026 tournament are leading to around 2.7 shots on goal per 30 minutes of ball-in-play time — an increase of around 34 per cent.

Those runs are effective because they cause tension in the opponents’ defence. Most often, that full-back will have their eye on a winger, while the centre-back on that side will be tracking the striker.

@ThomHarris_ 

🔗 nyti.ms/4yiUz3t

2076238007640719594.headline: World Cup
2076238007640719594.body: On this day, in 1998... The build-up to the 1998 World Cup final is perhaps more memorable than the game itself, with two separate Brazil team sheets appearing — the first with Ronaldo somehow not in the side and replaced by Edmundo, then a revised version with Ronaldo reinstated. It later emerged that Ronaldo had experienced a series of convulsions while having his afternoon nap, and had spent several hours in hospital. He arrived at the Stade de France separately from his team-mates. He eventually played the full 90 minutes, but was clearly lacking his usual spark. Amid these pre-match worries, Brazil had barely warmed up before the game, and never really got going. France dominated without playing spectacular football, and were 2-0 up at half-time thanks to Zinedine Zidane’s two headers, both after beating Leonardo to reach an inswinging corner. “Scoring two with my head is unbelievable,” he said afterwards. “I’m not very good with my head. But both times the ball came over perfectly for me, and I managed to connect perfectly.” There was little reaction from Brazil, even after Marcel Desailly’s red card on 68 minutes. 2-0 seemed a fair scoreline, but Emmanuel Petit added the icing on the cake after being slipped in by his Arsenal midfield colleague Patrick Vieira, to make it 3-0.
2076238007640719594.raw_text:
On this day, in 1998...

The build-up to the 1998 World Cup final is perhaps more memorable than the game itself, with two separate Brazil team sheets appearing — the first with Ronaldo somehow not in the side and replaced by Edmundo, then a revised version with Ronaldo reinstated.

It later emerged that Ronaldo had experienced a series of convulsions while having his afternoon nap, and had spent several hours in hospital. He arrived at the Stade de France separately from his team-mates.

He eventually played the full 90 minutes, but was clearly lacking his usual spark. Amid these pre-match worries, Brazil had barely warmed up before the game, and never really got going.

France dominated without playing spectacular football, and were 2-0 up at half-time thanks to Zinedine Zidane’s two headers, both after beating Leonardo to reach an inswinging corner. 

“Scoring two with my head is unbelievable,” he said afterwards. “I’m not very good with my head. But both times the ball came over perfectly for me, and I managed to connect perfectly.”

There was little reaction from Brazil, even after Marcel Desailly’s red card on 68 minutes. 

2-0 seemed a fair scoreline, but Emmanuel Petit added the icing on the cake after being slipped in by his Arsenal midfield colleague Patrick Vieira, to make it 3-0.

2076162735339892979.headline: Jude Bellingham
2076162735339892979.body: Thomas Tuchel is realising that England players are about heart as much as head. Mentality as much as method. How often have we had this debate down the decades? Welcome to England, Thomas. Cold-eyed analysis of England’s performance is required, of course, and Tuchel will study the quarter-final win with his staff. But he has to appreciate the nature and source of the adrenalin that fuels many England players. Tuchel was unhappy with the performance, and technical mistakes. His best player, Jude Bellingham, replied that it wasn’t technical or tactical issues but the psychological that underpinned England’s advance. xGuts. England players fought to get through against Norway whereas, judging by Tuchel’s reaction, he wants more thought. Wayne Rooney made the same point when England slunk out of Brazil in 2014. More intelligence required, “streetwise” was the word Rooney used. Argentina won the last World Cup with a blend of head and heart, and the genius that is Lionel Messi. England have Bellingham, a mix of savvy and spirit, technical and physical, all of it world-class, and he will surely relish looking for opportunities in an Argentinian defence that is not one of their better versions. Bellingham disputed Tuchel’s initial critique of England’s win. Tuchel balanced it in subsequent interviews. “I’m in love with my players,” Tuchel said. “I'm proud, I'm happy, and I feel so connected to this team, because they just do whatever it takes to take the next step. They just refuse to lose. They overcome obstacles and adversity.” There was a “but’ coming, a chunky caveat. “But I'm also a football coach, I also have demands that we want to bring out the best in us.” To improve performance. “Performance helps you win games. We can play better. It was not a high-level game. We made life complicated for ourself. The head of mine is not fully satisfied. We can play faster. We can play more clinical. Too many unforced errors and technical mistakes in our game. That cost us a lot of belief. We got lucky in decisive moments. But I am not saying that we are lucky to win.” Tuchel then opened up on his own emotional reaction to it all. “I feel very alive. I don’t want to be anywhere else in the world. It's a new level of emotional rollercoaster.” Welcome aboard, Thomas. ENG

Thomas Tuchel is realising that England players are about heart as much as head. Mentality as much as method. How often have we had this debate down the decades? Welcome to England, Thomas. Cold-eyed analysis of England’s performance is required, of course, and Tuchel will study the quarter-final win with his staff. But he has to appreciate the nature and source of the adrenalin that fuels many England players. 

Tuchel was unhappy with the performance, and technical mistakes. His best player, Jude Bellingham, replied that it wasn’t technical or tactical issues but the psychological that underpinned England’s advance. xGuts. 

England players fought to get through against Norway whereas, judging by Tuchel’s reaction, he wants more thought. Wayne Rooney made the same point when England slunk out of Brazil in 2014. More intelligence required, “streetwise” was the word Rooney used. 

Argentina won the last World Cup with a blend of head and heart, and the genius that is Lionel Messi. England have Bellingham, a mix of savvy and spirit, technical and physical, all of it world-class, and he will surely relish looking for opportunities in an Argentinian defence that is not one of their better versions.

Bellingham disputed Tuchel’s initial critique of England’s win. Tuchel balanced it in subsequent interviews. “I’m in love with my players,” Tuchel said. “I'm proud, I'm happy, and I feel so connected to this team, because they just do whatever it takes to take the next step. They just refuse to lose. They overcome obstacles and adversity.”

There was a “but’ coming, a chunky caveat. “But I'm also a football coach, I also have demands that we want to bring out the best in us.” To improve performance. “Performance helps you win games. We can play better. It was not a high-level game. We made life complicated for ourself. 

"The head of mine is not fully satisfied. We can play faster. We can play more clinical. Too many unforced errors and technical mistakes in our game. That cost us a lot of belief. We got lucky in decisive moments. But I am not saying that we are lucky to win.”

Tuchel then opened up on his own emotional reaction to it all. “I feel very alive. I don’t want to be anywhere else in the world. It's a new level of emotional rollercoaster.” Welcome aboard, Thomas. #ENG

2076326070387642573.headline: World Cup
2076326070387642573.body: 🏆 When it’s 64? “Send me a post, drop me a line, stating point of view.” POV - it’s controversial. Who could ask for more? FIFA. 64-team World Cup mooted by president Gianni Infantino as per TheAthleticFC. Surprise? No. Do the maths. And the broadcast/commercial booty. And the votes. 64 supposedly gives the group stage more edge - top two go through, none of the safety net for some thirds (who then get spanked). But weakens the group as 16 “lesser” added teams involved. Dilutes and undermines qualifying further. Club managers may withdraw players telling international coaches “you’re going to qualify anyway”. 64 also reduces the number of nations that can host, even co-host, plus impact on environment. But 64 will be popular amongst many national associations suddenly given a chance of qualification and additional revenue. And that inevitably strengthens support for Fifa president Gianni Infantino. FIFAWorldCup
2076326070387642573.raw_text:
🏆 When it’s 64? “Send me a post, drop me a line, stating point of view.” POV - it’s controversial. Who could ask for more? FIFA. 64-team World Cup mooted by president Gianni Infantino as per @TheAthleticFC. Surprise? No. Do the maths. And the broadcast/commercial booty. And the votes. 

64 supposedly gives the group stage more edge - top two go through, none of the safety net for some thirds (who then get spanked). But weakens the group as 16 “lesser” added teams involved. 

Dilutes and undermines qualifying further. Club managers may withdraw players telling international coaches “you’re going to qualify anyway”. 64 also reduces the number of nations that can host, even co-host, plus impact on environment. 

But 64 will be popular amongst many national associations suddenly given a chance of qualification and additional revenue. And that inevitably strengthens support for Fifa president Gianni Infantino. #FIFAWorldCup

2076435159449063875.headline: Joan Laporta
2076435159449063875.body: 🚨⚠️ Joan Laporta on Barça-Atletico talks: “The last time I spoke with Miguel Ángel Gil, we spoke with complete clarity and very well as usual, and I see that we have a very good relationship with them”. “There was some confusion at the beginning regarding the offer we made, and I clarified that for him”. “We didn't press further; I just told him that from the moment they have an alternative available, this offer will be valid, and that's where it stopped. It hasn't developed further than that at the moment”.
2076435159449063875.raw_text:
🚨⚠️ Joan Laporta on Barça-Atletico talks: “The last time I spoke with Miguel Ángel Gil, we spoke with complete clarity and very well as usual, and I see that we have a very good relationship with them”.

“There was some confusion at the beginning regarding the offer we made, and I clarified that for him”.

“We didn't press further; I just told him that from the moment they have an alternative available, this offer will be valid, and that's where it stopped. It hasn't developed further than that at the moment”.

2076400761647354288.headline: Mohammad Al
2076400761647354288.body: Worth noting that it’s rare for Mohammad Al-Kamali to hear cases as a sole arbiter. Martyn Ziegler (martynziegler) There are 18 members of FIFA's disciplinary committee but the unprecedented decision to lift Balogun's automatic ban was taken by just one person - the chairman Mohammad Al Kamali of UAE. No one else was consulted. Full story: thetimes.com/sport/football/… Link Only one official involved in decision to waive Folarin Balogun ban Mohammad al-Kamali, the chairman of Fifa’s disciplinary committee, made ruling without any of the 17 other members asked to be involved in the case thetimes.com —

2076380932970524727.headline: Johan Manzambi
2076380932970524727.body: Aston Villa are in the process of finalising a deal to sign midfielder Johan Manzambi from Freiburg. Manzambi looked on course to join Newcastle United after a club-to-club agreement was reached, but the 20-year-old had yet to decide on his preferred destination. That enabled Villa to accelerate their existing interest, which was heightened by the loss of Amadou Onana to a knee ligament injury. More from David_Ornstein ⬇️ 🔗 nyti.ms/454ySGV
2076380932970524727.raw_text:
Aston Villa are in the process of finalising a deal to sign midfielder Johan Manzambi from Freiburg.

Manzambi looked on course to join Newcastle United after a club-to-club agreement was reached, but the 20-year-old had yet to decide on his preferred destination.

That enabled Villa to accelerate their existing interest, which was heightened by the loss of Amadou Onana to a knee ligament injury.

More from @David_Ornstein ⬇️

🔗 nyti.ms/454ySGV

2076375549279093067.headline: Leaving Miami
2076375549279093067.body: Leaving Miami for Kansas City and then on to Atlanta. Whatever the understandable debate about performance etc the most significant and uplifting reality is that England are in the semi-finals of the World Cup for only the third time since 1966. And they have a world-class match-winner in Jude Bellingham and a world-class head coach in Thomas Tuchel. For those fans who have travelled all over supporting the team and being frustrated with early exits and good players underachieving … this World Cup is reward, a joyous journey, a rollercoaster for the emotions, a challenge for the nerves. And now … a semi-final awaits. ENG FIFAWorldCup
2076375549279093067.raw_text:
Leaving Miami for Kansas City and then on to Atlanta. Whatever the understandable debate about performance etc the most significant and uplifting reality is that England are in the semi-finals of the World Cup for only the third time since 1966. And they have a world-class match-winner in Jude Bellingham and a world-class head coach in Thomas Tuchel. 

For those fans who have travelled all over supporting the team and being frustrated with early exits and good players underachieving … this World Cup is reward, a joyous journey, a rollercoaster for the emotions, a challenge for the nerves. And now … a semi-final awaits. #ENG #FIFAWorldCup

deferred.headline: Michael Olise
deferred.body: Michael Olise is set to stay; no exit is planned at this stage.
deferred.text:
🚨❌ FC Bayern have also received confirmation from Michael Olise’s camp that there have been no talks, meetings or negotiations with Real Madrid so far.
No offer from Real Madrid either. Bayern are not open to negotiations. Olise has not expressed any desire to leave.
Instead, Bayern are working behind the scenes on contract extension beyond 2029 and want to make him one of the club’s top earners.  🇫🇷

deferred.headline: Jones
deferred.body: Jones is set to stay at Liverpool; no exit is planned at this stage.
deferred.text:
Inter made a fresh approach for Curtis Jones last week asking whether a deal was possible for around €25m.
This was a conversation not a formal or written offer.
Liverpool made it clear no sale at this price, especially given numbers other midfielders are expected to be sold for this summer.
Liverpool are prepared to keep Jones unless a suitable bid arises, even if that means losing him on a free next summer.
Liverpool have never had concerns about Jones' attitude or professionalism despite Inter reapproaching the club, and growing interest elsewhere.
Understand the club's position has hardened as a result of Inter's latest contact. If the right offer emerges, a sale will be sanctioned, but Inter would have to significantly increase their valuation to get Liverpool to engage.
Worth noting, Liverpool are also comfortable with Jones staying, and if that happens, and he impresses Andoni Iraola, they could yet decide to engage in new contract talks.🔴
deferred.draft_caption:
📝 REPORTED: Jones (Liverpool)
Jones is set to stay at Liverpool; no exit is planned at this stage.

#FPLVortex #Transfers #LFC #PremierLeague
